Money Stuff =0A I write a lot about private credit, and I talk sometimes wi=
th people running =0Aprivate credit at big asset managers, and this is very=
 embarras =0A=0A=0A <> =0A =0A<https://sli.bloomberg.com/click=3Fs=3D825881=
&stpe=3Ddefault&li=3D12527725&m=3D21d6dea82d6b16615cf44233be620525&p=3D0205=
2025>=0A=0A=0A=0APrivate credit=0A=0AI  write <> a  lot <> about private cr=
edit, and I talk sometimes with people =0Arunning private credit at big ass=
et managers, and this is very embarrassing, =0Abut I don=E2=80=99t know wha=
t private credit is=3F What makes something =E2=80=9Cprivate credit,=E2=80=
=9D =0Aas opposed to, uh, public credit=3F Here is my best approximation of=
 an answer. =0A[1]  <>=0A=0A * In public credit =E2=80=94 the common terms =
are =E2=80=9Cthe bond market=E2=80=9D and =E2=80=9Cthe =0Asyndicated loan m=
arket=E2=80=9D [2]  <> =E2=80=94 a company wants to borrow money, so it dea=

tuff and other =0Agreat Bloomberg newslettershere <>. Thanks!=0A=0A[1] This=
 is an effort mostly to distinguish things like =E2=80=9Ccorporate bonds=E2=
=80=9D and =0A=E2=80=9Csyndicated leveraged buyout financing=E2=80=9D from =
=E2=80=9Cprivate credit.=E2=80=9D There is another =0Aimportant form of =E2=
=80=9Cprivate credit=E2=80=9D <> for things like buy-now-pay-later loans =
=0Awhere you can think of it as being in competition with balance-sheet ban=
k =0Alending, where the difference is the funding model of the private cred=
it fund =0A(long-term, equity-funded) versus the bank (short-term, deposit-=
funded).=0A=0A[2] Private credit often consists of loans, and these days it=
 is often =0Asyndicated, so sometimes you will hear =E2=80=9Cbroadly syndic=
ated loans=E2=80=9D (or =E2=80=9Cbank =0Aloans=E2=80=9D) for the public kin=
d of loans.=0A=0A[3] Most notably, in a leveraged buyout, the banks will pr=
obably give you a =0Acommitment letter promising you the money, contemplati=
ng a marketing process =0Abut taking down the loan themselves if they have =
to. Generally in public-market =0Adebt commitments the banks will have some=
 =E2=80=9Cflex,=E2=80=9D where the terms on which they =0Aget stuck with th=
e debt are more buyer-friendly (higher interest rate, etc.) =0Athan the ter=
ms they hoped to market.=0A=0A[4] An important qualification to this paragr=
aph is that a lot of alternative =0Aasset managers, these days, also contro=
l insurance companies who provide them =0Awith a lot of their private credi=
t money, so in that sense they are principals, =0Ainvesting their insurance=
 floats. Though there too there is probably some =0Adistinction between the=
 insurer and the asset manager; in a sense the insurer =0Ais just a really =
big good client who really trusts the manager.=0A=0A[5] We  talked about th=
is distinction <> last year in the context of retail =0Aprivate *equity* fu=
nds. Broadly speaking institutional limited partners like =0Adrawdown funds=
 and SMAs where they don=E2=80=99t put up the money until you come to =0Ath=
em with a specific investment; with broad retail funds it is hard to do tha=
t =0Aand you will probably want to take the cash upfront.=0A=0A[6] It=E2=80=
=99s not essential that this be literally true. Different clients could =0A=
have different pre-set criteria (sector, size, rating, etc.) for their SMAs=
, so =0Athe manager will tap different clients for different loans. Or the =
manager =0Acould offer co-invests, upsizing, etc. The point is that the man=
ager mostly has =0Adiscretion to speak for client money.=0A=0A[7] One way i=
n which this is loose is that a lot of bond and syndicated loan =0Abuyers a=
re also funds run by asset managers. The difference is more that the =0Aass=
et manager in private credit is also the originator.=0A=0A[8] This one is a=
 little odd <>, because Musk owns only about 13% of Tesla=E2=80=99s =0Astoc=
k, depending how you count. He does not have voting control. But a Delaware=
 =0Ajudge has ruled that, given his shares and his superstar CEO status, he=
 is the =0Acontrolling shareholder <>.=0A=0A[9] The quotation marks are a l=
ittle fake here, and the actual term of art is =0A=E2=80=9Cthe entire fairn=
ess test.=E2=80=9D=0A=0A[10] Because you are the controlling shareholder. I=
t=E2=80=99s better if you have a =0Amajority-of-the-minority provision in t=
he vote, where you only move to Nevada =0Aif a majority of the outside shar=
eholders (not you) also vote in favor, though =0Athat is not entirely neces=
sary nor entirely sufficient.=0A=0A[11] He says in this video <> it was $27=
